title: Funeral
content:
آ آ آ آ The gods held talk together, groupï؟½d in knots,
آ آ آ آ Round Balderï؟½s corpse, which they had thither borne;
آ آ آ آ And Hermod came down towards them from the gate.
آ آ آ آ And Lok, the Father of the Serpent, first
آ آ آ آ Beheld him come, and to his neighbour spake:

آ آ آ آ ï؟½See, here is Hermod, who comes single back
آ آ آ آ From Hell; and shall I tell thee how he seems
آ آ آ آ Like as a farmer, who hath lost his dog,
آ آ آ آ Some morn, at market, in a crowded town
آ آ آ آ Through many streets the poor beast runs in vain,
آ آ آ آ And follows this man after that, for hours;
آ آ آ آ And, late at evening, spent and panting, falls
آ آ آ آ Before a strangerï؟½s threshold, not his home,
آ آ آ آ With flanks a-tremble, and his slender tongue
آ آ آ آ Hangs quivering out between his dust-smearï؟½d jaws,
آ آ آ آ And piteously he eyes the passers by:
آ آ آ آ But home his master comes to his own farm,
آ آ آ آ Far in the country, wondering where he is
آ آ آ آ So Hermod comes to-day unfollowï؟½d home.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ And straight his neighbour, movï؟½d with wrath, replied:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Deceiver, fair in form, but false in heart,
آ آ آ آ Enemy, Mocker, whom, though Gods, we hate
آ آ آ آ Peace, lest our Father Odin hear thee gibe.
آ آ آ آ Would I might see him snatch thee in his hand,
آ آ آ آ And bind thy carcase, like a bale, with cords,
آ آ آ آ And hurl thee in a lake, to sink or swim.
آ آ آ آ If clear from plotting Balderï؟½s death, to swim;
آ آ آ آ But deep, if thou devisedst it, to drown,
آ آ آ آ And perish, against fate, before thy day!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ So they two soft to one another spake.
آ آ آ آ But Odin lookï؟½d toward the land, and saw
آ آ آ آ His messenger; and he stood forth, and cried:
آ آ آ آ And Hermod came, and leapt from Sleipner down,
آ آ آ آ And in his Fatherï؟½s hand put Sleipnerï؟½s rein,
آ آ آ آ And greeted Odin and the Gods, and said:

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Odin, my Father, and ye, Gods of Heaven!
آ آ آ آ Lo, home, having performï؟½d your will, I come.
آ آ آ آ Into the joyless kingdom have I been,
آ آ آ آ Below, and lookï؟½d upon the shadowy tribes
آ آ آ آ Of ghosts, and communï؟½d with their solemn Queen;
آ آ آ آ And to your prayer she sends you this reply:
آ آ آ آ Show her through all the world the signs of grief:
آ آ آ آ Fails but one thing to grieve, there Balder stops.
آ آ آ آ Let Gods, men, brutes, beweep hint, plants and stones.
آ آ آ آ So shall she know your loss was dear indeed,
آ آ آ آ And bend her heart, and give you Balder back.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ He spoke; and all the Gods to Odin lookï؟½d:
آ آ آ آ And straight the Father of the Ages said:

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Ye Gods, these terms may keep another day.
آ آ آ آ But now, put on your arms, and mount your steeds,
آ آ آ آ And in procession all come near, and weep
آ آ آ آ Balder; for that is what the dead desire.
آ آ آ آ When ye enough have wept, then build a pile
آ آ آ آ Of the heapï؟½d wood, and burn his corpse with fire
آ آ آ آ Out of our sight; that we may turn from grief,
آ آ آ آ And lead, as erst, our daily life in Heaven.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ He spoke; and the Gods armï؟½d: and Odin donnï؟½d
آ آ آ آ His dazzling corslet and his helm of gold,
آ آ آ آ And led the way on Sleipner: and the rest
آ آ آ آ Followï؟½d, in tears, their Father and their King.
آ آ آ آ And thrice in arms around the dead they rode,
آ آ آ آ Weeping; the sands were wetted, and their arms,
آ آ آ آ With their thick-falling tears: so good a friend
آ آ آ آ They mournï؟½d that day, so bright, so lovï؟½d a God.
آ آ آ آ And Odin came, and laid his kingly hands
آ آ آ آ On Balderï؟½s breast, and thus began the wail:

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Farewell, O Balder, bright and lovï؟½d, my Son!
آ آ آ آ In that great day, the Twilight of the Gods.
آ آ آ آ When Muspelï؟½s children shall beleaguer Heaven,
آ آ آ آ Then we shall miss thy counsel and thy arm.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Thou camest near the next, O Warrior Thor!
آ آ آ آ Shouldering thy Hammer, in thy chariot drawn,
آ آ آ آ Swaying the long-hairï؟½d Goats with silverï؟½d rein;
آ آ آ آ And over Balderï؟½s corpse these words didst say:

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Brother, thou dwellest in the darksome land,
آ آ آ آ And talkest with the feeble tribes of ghosts,
آ آ آ آ Now, and I know not how they prize thee there,
آ آ آ آ But here, I know, thou wilt be missï؟½d and mournï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ For haughty spirits and high wraths are rife
آ آ آ آ Among the Gods and Heroes here in Heaven,
آ آ آ آ As among those, whose joy and work is war:
آ آ آ آ And daily strifes arise, and angry words:
آ آ آ آ But from thy lips, O Balder, night or day,
آ آ آ آ Heard no one ever an injurious word
آ آ آ آ To God or Hero, but thou keptest back
آ آ آ آ The others, labouring to compose their brawls.
آ آ آ آ Be ye then kind, as Balder too was kind:
آ آ آ آ For we lose him, who smoothï؟½d all strife in Heaven.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ He spake: and all the Gods assenting wailï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ And Freya next came nigh, with golden tears:
آ آ آ آ The loveliest Goddess she in Heaven, by all
آ آ آ آ Most honourï؟½d after Frea, Odinï؟½s wife:
آ آ آ آ Her long ago the wandering Oder took
آ آ آ آ To mate, but left her to roam distant lands;
آ آ آ آ Since then she seeks him, and weeps tears of gold:
آ آ آ آ Names hath she many; Vanadis on earth
آ آ آ آ They call her; Freya is her name in Heaven:
آ آ آ آ She in her hands took Balderï؟½s head, and spake:

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Balder, my brother, thou art gone a road
آ آ آ آ Unknown and long, and haply on that way
آ آ آ آ My long-lost wandering Oder thou hast met,
آ آ آ آ For in the paths of Heaven he is not found.
آ آ آ آ Oh, if it be so, tell him what thou wert
آ آ آ آ To his neglected wife, and what he is,
آ آ آ آ And wring his heart with shame, to hear thy word.
آ آ آ آ For he, my husband, left me here to pine,
آ آ آ آ Not long a wife, when his unquiet heart
آ آ آ آ First drove him from me into distant lands.
آ آ آ آ Since then I vainly seek him through the world,
آ آ آ آ And weep from shore to shore my golden tears,
آ آ آ آ But neither god nor mortal heeds my pain.
آ آ آ آ Thou only, Balder, wert for ever kind,
آ آ آ آ To take my hand, and wipe my tears, and say:
آ آ آ آ Weep not, O Freya, weep no golden tears!
آ آ آ آ One day the wandering Oder will return,
آ آ آ آ Or thou wilt find him in thy faithful search
آ آ آ آ On some great road, or resting in an inn,
آ آ آ آ Or at a ford, or sleeping by a tree.
آ آ آ آ So Balder said; but Oder, well I know,
آ آ آ آ My truant Oder I shall see no more
آ آ آ آ To the worldï؟½s end; and Balder now is gone;
آ آ آ آ And I am left uncomforted in Heaven.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ She spake; and all the Goddesses bewailï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Last, from among the Heroes one came near,
آ آ آ آ No God, but of the Hero-troop the chief
آ آ آ آ Regner, who swept the northern sea with fleets,
آ آ آ آ And rulï؟½d oï؟½er Denmark and the heathy isles,
آ آ آ آ Living; but Ella capturï؟½d him and slew:
آ آ آ آ A king, whose fame then fillï؟½d the vast of Heaven,
آ آ آ آ Now time obscures it, and menï؟½s later deeds:
آ آ آ آ He last approachï؟½d the corpse, and spake, and said:

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Balder, there yet are many Scalds in Heaven
آ آ آ آ Still left, and that chief Scald, thy brother Brage,
آ آ آ آ Whom we may bid to sing, though thou art gone:
آ آ آ آ And all these gladly, while we drink, we hear,
آ آ آ آ After the feast is done, in Odinï؟½s hall:
آ آ آ آ But they harp ever on one string, and wake
آ آ آ آ Remembrance in our soul of wars alone,
آ آ آ آ Such as on earth we valiantly have wagï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And blood, and ringing blows, and violent death:
آ آ آ آ But when thou sangest, Balder, thou didst strike
آ آ آ آ Another note, and, like a bird in spring,
آ آ آ آ Thy voice of joyance minded us, and youth,
آ آ آ آ And wife, and children, and our ancient home.
آ آ آ آ Yes, and I too rememberï؟½d then no more
آ آ آ آ My dungeon, where the serpents stung me dead,
آ آ آ آ Nor Ellaï؟½s victory on the English coast;
آ آ آ آ But I heard Thora laugh in Gothland Isle;
آ آ آ آ And saw my shepherdess, Aslauga, tend
آ آ آ آ Her flock along the white Norwegian beach:
آ آ آ آ Tears started to mine eyes with yearning joy
آ آ آ آ Therefore with grateful heart I mourn thee dead.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ So Regner spake, and all the Heroes groanï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ But now the sun had passï؟½d the height of Heaven,
آ آ آ آ And soon had all that day been spent in wail;
آ آ آ آ But then the Father of the Ages said:

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Ye Gods, there well may be too much of wail.
آ آ آ آ Bring now the gatherï؟½d wood to Balderï؟½s ship;
آ آ آ آ Heap on the deck the logs, and build the pyre.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ But when the Gods and Heroes heard, they brought
آ آ آ آ The wood to Balderï؟½s ship, and built a pile,
آ آ آ آ Full the deckï؟½s breadth, and lofty; then the corpse
آ آ آ آ Of Balder on the highest top they laid,
آ آ آ آ With Nanna on his right, and on his left
آ آ آ آ Hoder, his brother, whom his own hand slew.
آ آ آ آ And they set jars of wine and oil to lean
آ آ آ آ Against the bodies, and stuck torches near,
آ آ آ آ Splinters of pine-wood, soakï؟½d with turpentine;
آ آ آ آ And brought his arms and gold, and all his stuff,
آ آ آ آ And slew the dogs which at his table fed,
آ آ آ آ And his horse, Balderï؟½s horse, whom most he lovï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And threw them on the pyre, and Odin threw
آ آ آ آ A last choice gift thereon, his golden ring.
آ آ آ آ They fixt the mast, and hoisted up the sails,
آ آ آ آ Then they put fire to the wood; and Thor
آ آ آ آ Set his stout shoulder hard against the stern
آ آ آ آ To push the ship through the thick sand: sparks flew
آ آ آ آ From the deep trench she ploughï؟½d so strong a God
آ آ آ آ Furrowï؟½d it and the water gurgled in.
آ آ آ آ And the Ship floated on the waves, and rockï؟½d:
آ آ آ آ But in the hills a strong East-Wind arose,
آ آ آ آ And came down moaning to the sea; first squalls
آ آ آ آ Ran black oï؟½er the seaï؟½s face, then steady rushï؟½d
آ آ آ آ The breeze, and fillï؟½d the sails, and blew the fire.
آ آ آ آ And, wreathï؟½d in smoke, the Ship stood out to sea.
آ آ آ آ Soon with a roaring rose the mighty fire,
آ آ آ آ And the pile crackled; and between the logs
آ آ آ آ Sharp quivering tongues of flame shot out, and leapt,
آ آ آ آ Curling and darting, higher, until they lickï؟½d
آ آ آ آ The summit of the pile, the dead, the mast,
آ آ آ آ And ate the shrivelling sails; but still the Ship
آ آ آ آ Drove on, ablaze, above her hull, with fire.
آ آ آ آ And the Gods stood upon the beach, and gazï؟½d:
آ آ آ آ And, while they gazï؟½d, the Sun went lurid down
آ آ آ آ Into the smoke-wrapt sea, and Night came on.
آ آ آ آ Then the wind fell, with night, and there was calm.
آ آ آ آ But through the dark they watchï؟½d the burning Ship
آ آ آ آ Still carried oï؟½er the distant waters on
آ آ آ آ Farther and farther, like an Eye of Fire.
آ آ آ آ And as in the dark night a travelling man
آ آ آ آ Who bivouacs in a forest ï؟½mid the hills,
آ آ آ آ Sees suddenly a spire of flame shoot up
آ آ آ آ Out of the black waste forest, far below,
آ آ آ آ Which woodcutters have lighted near their lodge
آ آ آ آ Against the wolves; and all night long it flares:
آ آ آ آ So flarï؟½d, in the far darkness, Balderï؟½s pyre.
آ آ آ آ But fainter, as the stars rose high, it burnï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ The bodies were consumï؟½d, ash chokï؟½d the pile
آ آ آ آ And as in a decaying winter fire
آ آ آ آ A charrï؟½d log, falling, makes a shower of sparks
آ آ آ آ So, with a shower of sparks, the pile fell in,
آ آ آ آ Reddening the sea around; and all was dark.

آ آ آ آ But the Gods went by starlight up the shore
آ آ آ آ To Asgard, and sate down in Odinï؟½s hall
آ آ آ آ At table, and the funeral-feast began.
آ آ آ آ All night they ate the boar Serimnerï؟½s flesh,
آ آ آ آ And from their horns, with silver rimmï؟½d, drank mead,
آ آ آ آ Silent, and waited for the sacred Morn.

آ آ آ آ And Morning over all the world was spread.
آ آ آ آ Then from their loathï؟½d feast the Gods arose,
آ آ آ آ And took their horses, and set forth to ride
آ آ آ آ Oï؟½er the bridge Bifrost, where is Heimdallï؟½s watch,
آ آ آ آ To the ash Igdrasil, and Idaï؟½s plain:
آ آ آ آ Thor came on foot; the rest on horseback rode.
آ آ آ آ And they found Mimir sitting by his Fount
آ آ آ آ Of Wisdom, which beneath the ashtree springs;
آ آ آ آ And saw the Nornies watering the roots
آ آ آ آ Of that world-shadowing tree with Honey-dew:
آ آ آ آ There came the Gods, and sate them down on stones:
آ آ آ آ And thus the Father of the Ages said:

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Ye Gods, the terms ye know, which Hermod brought.
آ آ آ آ Accept them or reject them; both have grounds.
آ آ آ آ Accept them, and they bind us, unfulfillï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ To leave for ever Balder in the grave,
آ آ آ آ An unrecoverï؟½d prisoner, shade with shades.
آ آ آ آ But how, ye say, should the fulfilment fail?
آ آ آ آ Smooth sound the terms, and light to be fulfillï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ For dear-belovï؟½d was Balder while he livï؟½d
آ آ آ آ In Heaven and Earth, and who would grudge him tears?
آ آ آ آ But from the traitorous seed of Lok they come,
آ آ آ آ These terms, and I suspect some hidden fraud.
آ آ آ آ Bethink ye, Gods, is there no other way?
آ آ آ آ Speak, were not this a way, the way for Gods?
آ آ آ آ If I, if Odin, clad in radiant arms,
آ آ آ آ Mounted on Sleipner, with the Warrior Thor
آ آ آ آ Drawn in his car beside me, and my sons,
آ آ آ آ All the strong brood of Heaven, to swell my train,
آ آ آ آ Should make irruption into Helaï؟½s realm,
آ آ آ آ And set the fields of gloom ablaze with light,
آ آ آ آ And bring in triumph Balder back to Heaven?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ He spake; and his fierce sons applauded loud.
آ آ آ آ But Frea, Mother of the Gods, arose,
آ آ آ آ Daughter and wife of Odin; thus she said:

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Odin, thou Whirlwind, what a threat is this!
آ آ آ آ Thou threatenest what transcends thy might, even thine.
آ آ آ آ For of all powers the mightiest far art thou,
آ آ آ آ Lord over men on Earth, and Gods in Heaven;
آ آ آ آ Yet even from thee thyself hath been withheld
آ آ آ آ One thing; to undo what thou thyself hast rulï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ For all which hath been fixt, was fixt by thee:
آ آ آ آ In the beginning, ere the Gods were born,
آ آ آ آ Before the Heavens were builded, thou didst slay
آ آ آ آ The Giant Ymir, whom the Abyss brought forth,
آ آ آ آ Thou and thy brethren fierce, the Sons of Bor,
آ آ آ آ And threw his trunk to choke the abysmal void:
آ آ آ آ But of his flesh and members thou didst build
آ آ آ آ The Earth and Ocean, and above them Heaven:
آ آ آ آ And from the flaming world, where Muspel reigns,
آ آ آ آ Thou sentï؟½st and fetchedï؟½st fire, and madest lights,
آ آ آ آ Sun Moon and Stars, which thou hast hung in Heaven,
آ آ آ آ Dividing clear the paths of night and day:
آ آ آ آ And Asgard thou didst build, and Midgard Fort:
آ آ آ آ Then me thou madï؟½st; of us the Gods were born:
آ آ آ آ Then, walking by the sea, thou foundest spars
آ آ آ آ Of wood, and framedï؟½st men, who till the earth,
آ آ آ آ Or on the sea, the field of pirates, sail:
آ آ آ آ And all the race of Ymir thou didst drown,
آ آ آ آ Save one, Bergelmer; he on shipboard fled
آ آ آ آ Thy deluge, and from him the Giants sprang;
آ آ آ آ But all that brood thou hast removï؟½d far off,
آ آ آ آ And set by Oceanï؟½s utmost marge to dwell:
آ آ آ آ But Hela into Niflheim thou threwï؟½st,
آ آ آ آ And gavï؟½st her nine unlighted worlds to rule,
آ آ آ آ A Queen, and empire over all the dead.
آ آ آ آ That empire wilt thou now invade, light up
آ آ آ آ Her darkness, from her grasp a subject tear?
آ آ آ آ Try it; but I, for one, will not applaud.
آ آ آ آ Nor do I merit, Odin, thou shouldï؟½st slight
آ آ آ آ Me and my words, though thou be first in Heaven
آ آ آ آ For I too am a Goddess, born of thee,
آ آ آ آ Thine eldest, and of me the Gods are sprung;
آ آ آ آ And all that is to come I know, but lock
آ آ آ آ In my own breast, and have to none revealï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Come then; since Hela holds by right her prey,
آ آ آ آ But offers terms for his release to heaven,
آ آ آ آ Accept the chance; thou canst no more obtain.
آ آ آ آ Send through the world thy messengers: entreat
آ آ آ آ All living and unliving things to weep
آ آ آ آ For Balder; if thou haply thus mayï؟½st melt
آ آ آ آ Hela, and win the lovï؟½d one back to Heaven.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ She spake, and on her face let fall her veil,
آ آ آ آ And bowï؟½d her head, and sate with folded hands.
آ آ آ آ Nor did the all-ruling Odin slight her word;
آ آ آ آ Straightway he spake, and thus addressï؟½d the Gods:

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Go quickly forth through all the world, and pray
آ آ آ آ All living and unliving things to weep
آ آ آ آ Balder, if haply he may thus be won.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ When the Gods heard, they straight arose, and took
آ آ آ آ Their horses, and rode forth through all the world.
آ آ آ آ North south east west they struck, and roamï؟½d the world,
آ آ آ آ Entreating all things to weep Balderï؟½s death:
آ آ آ آ And all that livï؟½d, and all without life, wept.
آ آ آ آ And as in winter, when the frost breaks up,
آ آ آ آ At winterï؟½s end, before the spring begins,
آ آ آ آ And a warm west wind blows, and thaw sets in
آ آ آ آ After an hour a dripping sound is heard
آ آ آ آ In all the forests, and the soft-strewn snow
آ آ آ آ Under the trees is dibbled thick with holes.
آ آ آ آ And from the boughs the snowloads shuffle down;
آ آ آ آ And in fields sloping to the south dark plots
آ آ آ آ Of grass peep out amid surrounding snow,
آ آ آ آ And widen, and the peasantï؟½s heart is glad
آ آ آ آ So through the world was heard a dripping noise
آ آ آ آ Of all things weeping to bring Balder back:
آ آ آ آ And there fell joy upon the Gods to hear.

آ آ آ آ But Hermod rode with Niord, whom he took
آ آ آ آ To show him spits and beaches of the sea
آ آ آ آ Far off, where some unwarnï؟½d might fail to weep
آ آ آ آ Niord, the God of storms, whom fishers know
آ آ آ آ Not born in Heaven; he was in Vanheim rearï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ With men, but lives a hostage with the Gods:
آ آ آ آ He knows each frith, and every rocky creek
آ آ آ آ Fringï؟½d with dark pines, and sands where seafowl scream:
آ آ آ آ They two scourï؟½d every coast, and all things wept.
آ آ آ آ And they rode home together, through the wood
آ آ آ آ Of Jarnvid, which to east of Midgard lies
آ آ آ آ Bordering the Giants, where the trees are iron;
آ آ آ آ There in the wood before a cave they came
آ آ آ آ Where sate, in the caveï؟½s mouth, a skinny Hag,
آ آ آ آ Toothless and old; she gibes the passers by:
آ آ آ آ Thok is she callï؟½d; but now Lok wore her shape:
آ آ آ آ She greeted them the first, and laughï؟½d, and said:

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Ye Gods, good lack, is it so dull in Heaven.
آ آ آ آ That ye come pleasuring to Thokï؟½s Iron Wood?
آ آ آ آ Lovers of change ye are, fastidious sprites.
آ آ آ آ Look, as in some boorï؟½s yard a sweet-breathï؟½d cow
آ آ آ آ Whose manger is stuffï؟½d full of good fresh hay
آ آ آ آ Snuffs at it daintily, and stoops her head
آ آ آ آ To chew the straw, her litter, at her feet
آ آ آ آ So ye grow squeamish, Gods, and sniff at Heaven.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ She spake; but Hermod answerï؟½d her and said:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Thok, not for gibes we come, we come for tears.
آ آ آ آ Balder is dead, and Hela holds her prey,
آ آ آ آ But will restore, if all things give him tears.
آ آ آ آ Begrudge not thine; to all was Balder dear.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ But, with a louder laugh, the Hag replied:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Is Balder dead? and do ye come for tears?
آ آ آ آ Thok with dry eyes will weep oï؟½er Balderï؟½s pyre.
آ آ آ آ Weep him all other things, if weep they will
آ آ آ آ I weep him not: let Hela keep her prey!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ She spake; and to the cavernï؟½s depth she fled,
آ آ آ آ Mocking: and Hermod knew their toil was vain.
آ آ آ آ And as seafaring men, who long have wrought
آ آ آ آ In the great deep for gain, at last come home,
آ آ آ آ And towards evening see the headlands rise
آ آ آ آ Of their own country, and can clear descry
آ آ آ آ A fire of witherï؟½d furze which boys have lit
آ آ آ آ Upon the cliffs, or smoke of burning weeds
آ آ آ آ Out of a tillï؟½d field inland; then the wind
آ آ آ آ Catches them, and drives out again to sea:
آ آ آ آ And they go long days tossing up and down
آ آ آ آ Over the grey sea ridges; and the glimpse
آ آ آ آ Of port they had makes bitterer far their toil
آ آ آ آ So the Godsï؟½ cross was bitterer for their joy.

آ آ آ آ Then, sad at heart, to Niord Hermod spake:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½It is the Accuser Lok, who flouts us all.
آ آ آ آ Ride back, and tell in Heaven this heavy news.
آ آ آ آ I must again below, to Helaï؟½s realm.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ He spoke; and Niord set forth back to Heaven.
آ آ آ آ But northward Hermod rode, the way below;
آ آ آ آ Tho way he knew: and traversï؟½d Giallï؟½s stream,
آ آ آ آ And down to Ocean gropï؟½d, and crossï؟½d the ice,
آ آ آ آ And came beneath the wall, and found the grate
آ آ آ آ Still lifted; well was his return foreknown.
آ آ آ آ And once more Hermod saw around him spread
آ آ آ آ The joyless plains, and heard the streams of Hell.
آ آ آ آ But as he enterï؟½d, on the extremest hound
آ آ آ آ Of Niflheim, he saw one Ghost come near,
آ آ آ آ Hovering, and stopping oft, as if afraid;
آ آ آ آ Hoder, the unhappy, whom his own hand slew:
آ آ آ آ And Hermod lookï؟½d, and knew his brotherï؟½s ghost,
آ آ آ آ And callï؟½d him by his name, and sternly said:

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Hoder, ill-fated, blind in heart and eyes!
آ آ آ آ Why tarriest thou to plunge thee in the gulph
آ آ آ آ Of the deep inner gloom, but flittest here,
آ آ آ آ In twilight, on the lonely verge of Hell,
آ آ آ آ Far from the other ghosts, and Helaï؟½s throne?
آ آ آ آ Doubtless thou fearest to meet Balderï؟½s voice,
آ آ آ آ Thy brother, whom through folly thou didst slay.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ He spoke; but Hoder answerï؟½d him, and said:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Hermod the nimble, dost thou still pursue
آ آ آ آ The unhappy with reproach, even in the grave?
آ آ آ آ For this I died, and fled beneath the gloom,
آ آ آ آ Not daily to endure abhorring Gods,
آ آ آ آ Nor with a hateful presence cumber Heaven
آ آ آ آ And canst thou not, even here, pass pitying by?
آ آ آ آ No less than Balder have I lost the light
آ آ آ آ Of Heaven, and communion with my kin:
آ آ آ آ I too had once a wife, and once a child,
آ آ آ آ And substance, and a golden house in Heaven:
آ آ آ آ But all I left of my own act, and fled
آ آ آ آ Below, and dost thou hate me even here?
آ آ آ آ Balder upbraids me not, nor hates at all,
آ آ آ آ Though he has cause, have any cause; but he,
آ آ آ آ When that with downcast looks I hither came,
آ آ آ آ Stretchï؟½d forth his hand, and, with benignant voice,
آ آ آ آ Welcome, he said, if there be welcome here,
آ آ آ آ Brother and fellow-sport of Lok with me.
آ آ آ آ And not to offend thee, Hermod, nor to force
آ آ آ آ My hated converse on thee, came I up
آ آ آ آ From the deep gloom, where I will now return;
آ آ آ آ But earnestly I longï؟½d to hover near,
آ آ آ آ Not too far off, when that thou camest by,
آ آ آ آ To feel the presence of a brother God,
آ آ آ آ And hear the passage of a horse of Heaven,
آ آ آ آ For the last time: for here thou comï؟½st no more.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ He spake, and turnï؟½d to go to the inner gloom.
آ آ آ آ But Hermod stayï؟½d him with mild words, and said:

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Thou doest well to chide me, Hoder blind.
آ آ آ آ Truly thou sayï؟½st, the planning guilty mind
آ آ آ آ Was Lokï؟½s; the unwitting hand alone was thine.
آ آ آ آ But Gods are like the sons of men in this
آ آ آ آ When they have woe, they blame the nearest cause.
آ آ آ آ Howbeit stay, and be appeasï؟½d; and tell
آ آ آ آ Sits Balder still in pomp by Helaï؟½s side,
آ آ آ آ Or is he mingled with the unnumberï؟½d dead?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ And the blind Hoder answerï؟½d him and spake:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½His place of state remains by Helaï؟½s side,
آ آ آ آ But empty: for his wife, for Nanna came
آ آ آ آ Lately below, and joinï؟½d him; and the Pair
آ آ آ آ Frequent the still recesses of the realm
آ آ آ آ Of Hela, and hold converse undisturbï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ But they too doubtless, will have breathï؟½d the balm
آ آ آ آ Which floats before a visitant from Heaven,
آ آ آ آ And have drawn upwards to this verge of Hell.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ He spake; and, as he ceasï؟½d, a puff of wind
آ آ آ آ Rollï؟½d heavily the leaden mist aside
آ آ آ آ Round where they stood, and they beheld Two Forms
آ آ آ آ Make towards them oï؟½er the stretching cloudy plain.
آ آ آ آ And Hermod straight perceivï؟½d them, who they were,
آ آ آ آ Balder and Nanna; and to Balder said:

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Balder, too truly thou foresawï؟½st a snare.
آ آ آ آ Lok triumphs still, and Hela keeps her prey.
آ آ آ آ No more to Asgard shalt thou come, nor lodge
آ آ آ آ In thy own house, Breidablik, nor enjoy
آ آ آ آ The love all bear towards thee, nor train up
آ آ آ آ Forset, thy son, to be belovï؟½d like thee.
آ آ آ آ Here must thou lie, and wait an endless age.
آ آ آ آ Therefore for the last time, O Balder, hail!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ He spake; and Balder answerï؟½d him and said:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Hail and farewell, for here thou comï؟½st no more.
آ آ آ آ Yet mourn not for me, Hermod, when thou sittï؟½st
آ آ آ آ In Heaven, nor let the other Gods lament,
آ آ آ آ As wholly to be pitied, quite forlorn:
آ آ آ آ For Nanna hath rejoinï؟½d me, who, of old,
آ آ آ آ In Heaven, was seldom parted from my side;
آ آ آ آ And still the acceptance follows me, which crowned
آ آ آ آ My former life, and cheers me even here.
آ آ آ آ The iron frown of Hela is relaxï؟½d
آ آ آ آ When I draw nigh, and the wan tribes of dead
آ آ آ آ Trust me, and gladly bring for my award
آ آ آ آ Their ineffectual feuds and feeble hates,
آ آ آ آ Shadows of hates, but they distress them still.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ And the fleet-footed Hermod made reply:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Thou hast then all the solace death allows,
آ آ آ آ Esteem and function: and so far is well.
آ آ آ آ Yet here thou liest, Balder, underground,
آ آ آ آ Rusting for ever: and the years roll on,
آ آ آ آ The generations pass, the ages grow,
آ آ آ آ And bring us nearer to the final day
آ آ آ آ When from the south shall march the Fiery Band
آ آ آ آ And cross the Bridge of Heaven, with Lok for guide,
آ آ آ آ And Fenris at his heel with broken chain:
آ آ آ آ While from the east the Giant Rymer steers
آ آ آ آ His ship, and the great Serpent makes to land;
آ آ آ آ And all are marshallï؟½d in one flaming square
آ آ آ آ Against the Gods, upon the plains of Heaven.
آ آ آ آ I mourn thee, that thou canst not help us then.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ He spake; but Balder answerï؟½d him and said:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Mourn not for me: Mourn, Hermod, for the Gods:
آ آ آ آ Mourn for the men on Earth, the Gods in Heaven,
آ آ آ آ Who live, and with their eyes shall see that day.
آ آ آ آ The day will come, when Asgardï؟½s towers shall fall,
آ آ آ آ And Odin, and his Sons, the seed of Heaven:
آ آ آ آ But what were I, to save them in that hour?
آ آ آ آ If strength could save them, could not Odin save,
آ آ آ آ My Father, and his pride, the Warrior Thor,
آ آ آ آ Vidar the Silent, the Impetuous Tyr?
آ آ آ آ I, what were I, when these can naught avail?
آ آ آ آ Yet, doubtless. when the day of battle comes,
آ آ آ آ And the two Hosts are marshallï؟½d, and in Heaven
آ آ آ آ The golden-crested Cock shall sound alarm,
آ آ آ آ And his black Brother-Bird from hence reply,
آ آ آ آ And bucklers clash, and spears begin to pour
آ آ آ آ Longing will stir within my breast, though vain.
آ آ آ آ But not to me so grievous, as, I know,
آ آ آ آ To other Gods it were, is my enforcï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Absence from fields where I could nothing aid:
آ آ آ آ For I am long since weary of your storm
آ آ آ آ Of carnage, and find, Hermod, in your life
آ آ آ آ Something too much of war and broils, which make
آ آ آ آ Life one perpetual fight, a bath of blood.
آ آ آ آ Mine eyes are dizzy with the arrowy hail;
آ آ آ آ Mine ears are stunnï؟½d with blows, and sick for calm.
آ آ آ آ Inactive therefore let me lie, in gloom,
آ آ آ آ Unarmï؟½d, inglorious: I attend the course
آ آ آ آ Of ages, and my late return to light,
آ آ آ آ In times less alien to a spirit mild,
آ آ آ آ In new-recoverï؟½d seats, the happier day.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ He spake; and the fleet Hermod thus replied:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Brother, what seats are these, what happier day?
آ آ آ آ Tell me, that I may ponder it when gone.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ And the ray-crowned Balder answerï؟½d him:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Far to the south, beyond The Blue, there spreads
آ آ آ آ Another Heaven, The Boundless: no one yet

آ آ آ آ Hath reachï؟½d it: there hereafter shall arise
آ آ آ آ The second Asgard, with another name.
آ آ آ آ Thither, when oï؟½er this present Earth and Heavens
آ آ آ آ The tempest of the latter days hath swept,
آ آ آ آ And they from sight have disappearï؟½d, and sunk,
آ آ آ آ Shall a small remnant of the Gods repair:
آ آ آ آ Hoder and I shall join them from the grave.
آ آ آ آ There re-assembling we shall see emerge
آ آ آ آ From the bright Ocean at our feet an Earth
آ آ آ آ More fresh, more verdant than the last, with fruits
آ آ آ آ Self-springing, and a seed of man preservï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Who then shall live in peace, as now in war.
آ آ آ آ But we in Heaven shall find again with joy
آ آ آ آ The ruinï؟½d palaces of Odin, seats
آ آ آ آ Familiar, halls where we have suppï؟½d of old;
آ آ آ آ Re-enter them with wonder, never fill
آ آ آ آ Our eyes with gazing, and rebuild with tears.
آ آ آ آ And we shall tread once more the well-known plain
آ آ آ آ Of Ida, and among the grass shall find
آ آ آ آ The golden dice with which we playï؟½d of yore;
آ آ آ آ And that will bring to mind the former life
آ آ آ آ And pastime of the Gods, the wise discourse
آ آ آ آ Of Odin, the delights of other days.
آ آ آ آ O Hermod, pray that thou mayst join us then!
آ آ آ آ Such for the future is my hope: meanwhile,
آ آ آ آ I rest the thrall of Hela, and endure
آ آ آ آ Death, and the gloom which round me even now
آ آ آ آ Thickens, and to its inner gulph recalls.
آ آ آ آ Farewell, for longer speech is not allowï؟½d.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ He spoke, and wavï؟½d farewell, and gave his hand
آ آ آ آ To Nanna; and she gave their brother blind
آ آ آ آ Her hand, in turn, for guidance; and The Three
آ آ آ آ Departed oï؟½er the cloudy plain, and soon
آ آ آ آ Faded from sight into the interior gloom.
آ آ آ آ But Hermod stood beside his drooping horse,
آ آ آ آ Mute, gazing after them in tears: and fain,
آ آ آ آ Fain had he followï؟½d their receding steps,
آ آ آ آ Though they to Death were bound, and he to Heaven,
آ آ آ آ Then; but a Power he could not break withheld.
آ آ آ آ And as a stork which idle boys have trappï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And tied him in a yard, at autumn sees
آ آ آ آ Flocks of his kind pass flying oï؟½er his head
آ آ آ آ To warmer lands, and coasts that keep the sun;
آ آ آ آ He strains to join their flight, and, from his shed,
آ آ آ آ Follows them with a long complaining cry
آ آ آ آ So Hermod gazï؟½d, and yearnï؟½d to join his kin.

آ آ آ آ At last he sighï؟½d, and set forth back to Heaven.
meaning:
"Funeral" is a quintessential example of Matthew Arnold's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
